pages.text: Assessing drying shrinkage and water permeability of reactive powder concrete produced in Hong Kong C.M. Tam a, Vivian W.Y. Tam b,⇑, K.M. Ng a a Department of Building & Construction, City University of Hong Kong, 83 Tat Chee Avenue, Kowloon, Hong Kong b School of Engineering, University of Western Sydney, Locked Bag 1797, Penrith South DC, NSW 1797, Australia article info Article history: Received 8 November 2010 Received in revised form 30 March 2011 Accepted 23 May 2011 Keywords: Reactive powder concrete Durability Drying shrinkage Water permeability Hong Kong abstract Reactive powder concrete (RPC) is coarse aggregate-free which differs from that of the ordinary concrete. Instead, ﬁne powders such as quartz sand and crushed quartz, with particle sizes ranging from 45 to 600 lm are used. In fact, it is rather a mortar than a concrete mixture because of the lack of coarse aggre- gate. The mechanical property performance of RPC has been investigated by the previous researchers. However, the performance on drying shrinkage and water permeability is of paucity in the literatures. This paper examines the inﬂuences of water-to-binder ratio and superplasticizer dosage on the drying shrinkage and water permeability of RPC. Recommendations for reducing drying shrinkage and water permeability of RPC are also discussed. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Exciting new developments have been recorded in the area of cementitious materials such as the recent innovations in high strength concrete (HSC), high performance concrete (HPC) and ultra-high strength (performance) concrete (UHSC or UHPC). With the development of these concrete technologies, these high-tech materials provide great application potentials to the construction ﬁeld. Reactive powder concrete (RPC) is coarse aggregate-free concrete, which has limited applications so far recorded in the construction industry. RPC was originally developed by Rhodia, Lafarge and Bouygues, the parent company of VSL, and is a regis- tered trademark under the name of Ductal [1], which reported that the durability properties of RPC were in multiple folds of magni- tude better than the current high performance concrete. The ultra-high performance of RPC provides many advantages compared to the conventional concrete; such as: (i) superior strengths with very high compressive strength of 200 MPa (approximately four times the strengths of conven- tional concrete) result in signiﬁcant reduction in dead load. RPC structures may weigh only one-third or one-half of the corresponding conventional concrete structures. Weight reduction is good in producing more slender transportation structures, reducing overall costs and increasing usable ﬂoor space in high-rise buildings [1–3]; (ii) superior ductility and energy absorption (typical values of 300 times greater than that of HPC and is comparable to those of some metals) provide greater structure reliability even under overload conditions or earthquakes [2–4]; the high energy absorption characteristics of RPC may also allow improved post-elastic response of columns, beam-column joints and shear walls [5]; (iii) superior durability which leads to long service life with reduced maintenance. RPC is nearly impermeable, allowing almost no carbonation or penetration of chlorides and sul- phates. The enhanced abrasion resistance provides extended life for bridge decks and industrial ﬂoors [3]; while the enhanced corrosion resistance provides protec

3.2.3. Properties of cement Permeability of concrete is also affected by the properties of ce- ment [24]. With the same water-to-cement ratios, coarse cement tends to produce a hardened cement paste with a higher porosity than ﬁner cement. In order to study the performance of RPC in terms of drying shrinkage and water permeability, a series of experimental tests have been designed, which are described in the following sections. 4. Experimental design 4.1. Materials In this paper, constituents used in preparing the RPC mixtures are different from the conventional concrete mixtures. The materials include Ordinary Portland Ce- ment, silica fume, quartz sand, crushed quartz, superplasticizer (SP) and water. De- tails of each constituent are recapitulated as follows. 4.1.1. Ordinary Portland Cement The cement used throughout the experiments is Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC) which complies with British Standards [26,27] and has a 28-day mortar compressive strength of 58 MPa. The density is 3120 kg/m 3 and the ﬁneness is 3390 cm2/g. The initial and ﬁnal setting times are 140 min and 165 min respec- tively. The chemical composition of the cement is given in Table 2 . 4.1.2. Silica fume The silica fume used is called Sikafume from Sika (Hong Kong) Limited which complies with ASTM standard [28]. The silica fume is extremely ﬁne with particle size of 0.1 lm, which exists in grey powder form and contains latently reactive sil- icon dioxide and no chloride or other potentially corrosive substances. The dry bulk density is 0.65 + 0.1 kg. The recommended dosage ranges from 2% to 10% of cement content by weight. For optimum results in concrete, it is suggested to use in con- junction with Sikament SP, a superplasticizer. 4.1.3. Quartz sand The majority of mixes are produced using quartz sand which replaces coarse aggregate from the conventional concrete. The quartz sand is supplied by Fantasy Quartz Sand Supply Company in Hong Kong. It is in a form of yellowish-white high purity silica sand. 4.1.4. Crushed quartz The crushed quartz used in the experiments is in a form of white powdered quartz ﬂour which replaces ﬁne aggregate from the conventional concrete. The quartz ﬂour is also supplied by Fantasy Quartz Sand Supply Company in Hong Kong. The particle size used ranges from 10 to 45 lm. 4.1.5. Superplasticizer (SP) The very low water-to-binder ratio used in RPC is only possible with the use of SP for obtaining its workability. In this paper, the 3rd generation of SP called Sika- ment ViscoCrete – 1250NT from Sika (Hong Kong) Limited is used. It is an extremely high water-reducing agent that meets the requirements for SPs according to British Standard [29]. Descriptions are provided in Table 3 . 4.2. Mix proportions and curing conditions In this study, a RPC mix is designed. Table 4 provides the details of the RPC mix design which is based on some published recommended compositions [30–33]. The RPC mixes are produced using a high-speed mixer of volume 0.008 m 3 with a max- imum speed of about 1800 rpm. The objective of this paper is to investigate drying shrinkage and water perme- ability for RPC. Two RPC mix series with different water-to-binder ratios and SP dosages are produced for drying shrinkage tests and water penetration tests. Differ- ent water-to-binder ratios, including 0.17, 0.2, 0.23, and 0.4 are used by keeping the SP dosage constant at 2.5%; whereas to study the effect of SP dosage on drying shrinkage and water permeability for RPC, SP d

volume of porosities, in which the emphasis shifts towards isolated pores that are not connected to the outer boundaries of the RPC. However, viscosity is found to signiﬁcantly decrease with increas- ing SP dosage. When excess SP dosage is added, the RPC would be associated with large slump loss and the problem of segregation would occur. More air would be trapped during mixing which may lead to higher porosity and consequently higher water permeability. It is found that the inﬂuence of superplasticizers on water per- meability of RPC is more signiﬁcant at an early age while the inﬂu- ence is not signiﬁcant in the long term. It is also noted that the curve turns ﬂat at the later age with increasing SP dosage, regard- less of the optimal SP dosage of 2.5% in which the permeability coefﬁcient remains constant at 28 days. The permeability coefﬁ- cient for RPC with SP dosage of 2% is almost constant at 42 days, whereas for RPC with SP dosages of 3% and 3.5%, it takes 56 days and 90 days respectively for the permeability coefﬁcient to become constant. This may be due to the delayed initial hydration of ce- ment when SP dosage is increased which results from excessive retardation of the setting time. Superpalsticizers would delay the initial hydration of cement but they hardly inﬂuence the hydration reaction at the later stage [65]. It is believed that the delayed hydration of cement would cause delay for the formation of sufﬁ- cient C–S–H or other hydration products to block the capillary pores. Therefore, it takes longer time for the permeability coefﬁ- cient of RPC to become steady when higher SP dosage is incorporated. 5.2.3. Relationship between strength and permeability of RPC It has been reported that strength and permeability are inver- sely proportional to the volume of large pores in the hydrated ce- ment paste, the considerable pore reﬁnement leads to signiﬁcant increase in impermeability of concrete [66]. The relationship be- tween compressive strength and water permeability of RPC under various ages is shown in Fig. 9 . From Fig. 9 , it is observed that water permeability of RPC de- creases as compressive strength increases across the age. It is also noted from Fig. 9 that the longer the curing period, the greater the ultimate compressive strength and the lower the corresponding water permeability are obtained. The compressive strength of RPC grows over a lengthy period. The most rapid increase can be observed during the initial period of hardening up to 28 days and then the rate gradually decreases with age. On the other hand, the permeability of RPC is adversely affected by its age. The perme- ability coefﬁcient after 3 days of curing equals about 11 times the permeability coefﬁcient after 90 days of curing. This is attributed to the completion of hydration process which leads to the increase in the volume of gel and hydration products and hence decreases the voids in RPC. It is known that both compressive strength and water perme- ability are largely inﬂuenced by water-to-binder ratios. Fig. 10 shows the relationship between compressive strength and water permeability of RPC with different water-to-binder ratios. From Fig. 10 , it is found that the water-to-binder ratio of 0.2 gives the maximum compressive strength and minimum water permeability of RPC. Beyond this optimal water-to-binder ratio, increasing the water-to-binder ratio decreases the compressive strength and increases the water permeability of RPC due to high amoun

6. Conclusion This paper has investigated the drying shrinkage behaviour and water permeability of RPC. Different water-to-binder ratios and SP dosages were experimented. It was found that RPC generally exhibits relatively low dying shrinkage. This may be partly due to the reduction of the pore size and connectivity of the voids which results from the high packing density of RPC and partly due to the prestressing effect of silica fume which slows down the water evaporation and restrains the shrinkage. The higher the water-to-binder ratios, the higher the shrinkage strains of RPC will be. This may be due to the greater disequilibrium in the relative humidity between concrete and the external drying envi- ronment which leads to higher evaporation and more pores formed at higher water-to-binder ratio which develops higher surface ten- sion in pores that causes concrete to shrink. Higher SP dosages give higher drying shrinkage strains and shrinkage rate development. This is because high SP dosage would cause changes in the pore structure which inﬂuences the state of the evaporable water and consequently the shrinkage behaviour. Microcracks in RPC were observed under SEM which is possibly due to the self-desiccating action. Incorporation of shrinkage reducing admixture can help re- duce cracks. It was also found that RPC generally exhibits very low water permeability, with the permeability coefﬁcient typically lower than that of the normal concrete by an order of magnitude. This may be attributed to the homogenous and dense microstructure of RPC which minimizes the porosity by ﬁller effects of silica fume and crushed quartz that leads to disconnected pores and results in a very low permeability. Water permeability of RPC decreases as the water-to-binder ratio decreases. The permeability coefﬁcient lowers by 2 orders of magnitude when water-to-binder ratio de- creases from 0.4 to 0.2 at 105 days. This may be attributed to the small pores found with low water-to-binder ratio and gross vol- ume of C–S–H gel and crystals formed which block and disconnect the capillary pores. The effect of SP dosage is not as signiﬁcant as that of water-to-binder ratio on water permeability of RPC. How- ever, an optimal SP dosage can reduce the water content and amount of pores, and help disperse the material particles better which results in a very dense and compacted paste with low poros- ity. Insufﬁcient SP dosage would make compaction difﬁcult and lead to high level of porosity and interconnected pores predomi- nate. Excessive SP dosage can lead to chemical incompatibility problems and segregation may occur, resulting in higher porosity and consequently higher water permeability. Inﬂuence of SP on water permeability of RPC is more signiﬁcant at the early age than in the long term. This is because superpalsticizers would delay the initial hydration of cement which delays the formation of sufﬁcient C–S–H or other hydration products to block the capillary pores; but they hardly inﬂuence the hydration reaction at the later stage. There is an inverse relationship between strength and permeability of RPC. It was found that water permeability of RPC decreases as compressive strength increases. Acknowledgement The work described in this paper was fully supported by a stra- tegic research grant from the City University of Hong Kong. References [1] Rebentrost M, Cavill B. Reactive powder concrete bridges. In: Austroads 6th bridge conference: bridging the gap. Perth, We

Though RPC possesses many outstanding properties, they also have certain weaknesses; for example: (i) cement content as high as 800–1000 kg/m 3 not only affects the production costs, but also has negative effects on the heat of hydration, causing shrinkage problems [7]; and (ii) RPC is generally costly and cannot replace the conventional concrete in most applications where the conventional mixes can economically meet the performance criteria [3]. This paper aims to achieve the following objectives: /C15to investigate factors affecting the drying shrinkage and water permeability behaviour of RPC; /C15to examine the inﬂuences of water-to-binder ratio and superp- lasticizer dosage on drying shrinkage and water permeability of RPC; and /C15to recommend methods for improving the behaviour of RPC. 2. Theoretical background on shrinkage of concrete Shrinkage of concrete is caused by loss of water due to evapora- tion or by chemical change resulted from hydration of cement, as well as by carbonation. In general, shrinkage of concrete is believed to take place in the cement paste matrix. There are many types of shrinkages, in which drying shrinkage, autogenous shrinkage and chemical shrinkage are the most common ones. Although some other types of shrinkage, such as carbonation shrinkage, are also present, they contribute very little to the overall shrinkage and may be neglected [8]. Shrinkage is the major cause of cracking in concrete, so it should be carefully monitored. 2.1. Drying shrinkage Drying shrinkage of concrete is the reduction in volume caused by the withdrawal of water from hardened concrete stored in unsaturated air to the surroundings [8]. It is also deﬁned as the time-dependent strain measured at constant temperature in an unloaded and unrestrained specimen [9]. Shrinkage presents the greatest value at the member surfaces exposed to drying and de- creases towards the interior of the member [9]. If drying conditions are the same at both top and bottom surfaces of the specimen, the total strain would be uniform over the depth of the specimen; however, if drying occurs at a different rate from the top and bot- tom surfaces, the total strain distribution becomes inclined and warping of the member would result. Drying shrinkage induces tension and results in the formation of cracks, leading to service- ability and durability problems. Factors that affect drying shrink- age of concrete include constituents, porosity, age of concrete, curing temperature, relative humidity, moisture content and types of admixtures used [10,11]. 2.2. Autogenous shrinkage Autogenous shrinkage is the apparent volume (sum of volumes of its different components such as solid, liquid or gaseous phases) reduction of cementitious materials by cement hydration [12,13]. However, it does not include volume change due to loss or ingress of substances, temperature variation, application of an external force and restraint [14]. When there is no additional water beyond that added during mixing is provided, the concrete would begin to dry out even if no moisture is lost to the surroundings. Both water- to-cement ratio and the incorporation of silica fume have signiﬁ- cantly effects on the autogenous shrinkage of concrete [15], which is due to the internal consumption of water during hydration, which leads to autogenous shrinkage [11]. The autogenous shrinkage increases with decreasing water to cement ratios and with increasing silica fume content [14–17]. Autogenous shr

4.3. Mixing sequence An important factor for studying these new cementitious materials is the mix- ing procedure [34]. This inﬂuence is often neglected and might be a source of error when analyzing experimental results. Since RPC is composed of very ﬁne materials, the conventional mixing method is not appropriate and the mixing method cannot be the same. The following sequence in mixing RPC is based on the previous studies [5,32,35–38], as well as trial-and-error approaches from the research team: 1. dry mixing powders (including cement, quartz sand, crushed quartz and silica fume) for about 1 min at a constant speed of 1800 rpm; 2. addition of half volume of water containing half amount of SP; 3. mixing for about 3 min with a speed of about 285 rpm; 4. addition of the remaining water and SP; and 5. mixing for about 8 min at a constant speed of 1800 rpm. The entire mixing process takes about 12–16 min. When RPC mix is ready, it is poured into the required moulds which have been sprayed with mould oil to reduce the friction at the interface between the moulds and RPC mix. The RPC mix is com- pacted using a vibrating table and hand tamping using a square tamping rod. The cast moulds are covered by plastic sheets until demoulding to prevent moisture in the concrete from losing to the surroundings. The specimens are demoulded at least 24 h after casting because of the high SP dosage which requires longer setting time. 4.4. Testing 4.4.1. Drying shrinkage The shrinkage measuring apparatus used to determine the shrinkage strain for RPC is the concrete length comparator as shown in Fig. 1 . The comparator is com- prised of an upright support of 14 00 (356 mm) tall attached to a metal base and including a 600 (152 mm) reference bar. It is designed to quickly and accurately mea- sure the height of concrete samples to within ±0.1% of the total height. A digital indicator to an accuracy of within 0.0001 in. (0.002 mm) with 0 to 1 00 (0 to 25 mm) total range is used. The shrinkage strain is calculated according to the ASTM standard [39]. 4.4.2. Water permeability To assess the water permeability of the concrete, Germann’s Water Permeabil- ity testing device GWT – 4000 Kit developed by Germann Instruments A/S is used. The test is carried out in accordance with the Manual [40]. The GWT is used for test- ing micro-cracking and porosities of the concrete surface, the ‘‘skin concrete’’ by applying water pressure to the surface and observe how the water ﬂows. The differ- ence in the gauge position over a given time (e.g. 5 min) is taken as a measure of the water permeability for the water pressure. The GWT allows testing up to a maxi- mum of 6 bar pressure. Testing is performed on a horizontal face. The set up is illus- trated in Fig. 2. The attachment of the GWT unit is by means of two clamping pliers anchored to the surface. 4.4.3. Microstructure and chemical composition Microstructure and chemical composition of RPC are examined using a JSM – 820 Scanning Electronic Microscope ﬁtted with an Energy Dispersive X-ray analyz- ing facility. 5. Results and discussions 5.1. Drying shrinkage Drying shrinkages of RPC with different water-to-binder ratios and SP dosages are measured at speciﬁc days. The result is summa- rized in Table 6 . 5.1.1. Effect of water-to-binder ratio on shrinkage behaviour of RPC There are various factors inﬂuencing the drying shrinkage of hardened concrete. Water content is probably the largest single factor inﬂuencing the shrinkage

572 and 619 microstrains for water-to-binder ratios of 0.17, 0.20 and 0.23 respectively. It is also noted that the shrinkage rate of RPC at the early age (ﬁrst 14 days) increases with increasing water-to-binder ratios. Particularly for RPC with high water-to- binder ratio of 0.4 which shows rapid increase in initial shrinkage. The high drying shrinkage strain and shrinkage rate for RPC with high water-to-binder ratios are mainly due to disequilibrium in the relative humidity between concrete and its environment. More water is lost due to evaporation and thus concrete shrinks more, particular at the early age. Another reason may be attributed to the surface tension that develops in small pores as water evapo- rates. When cement reacts with water, C–S–H forms in water-ﬁlled spaces [45]. These spaces are not completely ﬁlled by the C–S–H and so a capillary pore network develops. As water evaporates, a meniscus will be formed among the pores. The surface tension of the water pulls the pore walls inward causing the concrete to shrink. Therefore, the high drying shrinkage strain for RPC with high water-to-binder ratio maybe due to more pores formed at (a) (b) Top knob of plier Pressure chamber Pressure meter Micrometer gauge Anchoring tools (G-clamp) Water filling cup with L-joint Tested Concrete Fig. 2. higher water-to-binder ratios which develop higher surface ten- sion in pores. It is interesting to point out that although the drying shrinkage is lower for the lower water-to-binder mixture, the total shrinkage could be higher at early and medium ages due to larger autogenous shrinkage in low water-to-binder mixture [46]. It is known that high strength concrete with low water-to-binder ratios can induce autogenous shrinkage because curing water cannot reach the internal small pores and thus results in internal drying which is called self-desiccation [47]. However, for concrete with higher water-to-binder ratios, autogenous shrinkage is usually small be- cause the concrete has enough water supplied into pores during curing period for hydration [48,49]. Concrete with a low water- to-binder ratio, autogenous shrinkage would be the dominant cause whereas for concrete with a high water-to-binder ratio, evaporation is the governing cause [50]. Therefore, it can be con- cluded that low water-to-binder ratios for RPC has low drying shrinkage but high autogenous shrinkage while high water-to-bin- der ratios for RPC has high drying shrinkage but low autogenous shrinkage. 5.1.2. Effect of superplasticizer dosage on shrinkage behaviour for RPC Superplasticizers are chemical admixtures that are incorporated into the concrete to increase the workability in its fresh state. The incorporation of chemical admixtures is known to affect the poros- ity of the hardened cement paste, both the total porosity and the pore size distribution [51], which consequently affects the shrink- age behaviour of the concrete. Fig. 4 shows the drying shrinkage development of RPC with different SP dosages under constant water-to-binder ratio of 0.2 within 4 months of about 130 days. Taking SP dosage of 2.5%, its drying shrinkage varies between 167 and 572 microstrain within 130 days. Many investigations showed that signiﬁcant SP dosage is needed to decrease the amount of water required without loss of workability [52,53]. The action of these high-range water reducers mainly derives from a better particle dispersion as admixture is adsorbed on the cement particles and hence, reduction of the surface tension of water re- sults in lower capillary pressure which consequently decreases the shrinkage strain [54]. The ultimate shrinkage strains of RPC with 3% and 3.5% SP dos- ages are 863 and 1148 microstrains respectively, which are signif- icantly higher when compared with other two SP dosages, which are 501 and 572 microstrains for SP dosages of 2% and 2.5% respectively. The high drying shrinkage strain and shrinkage rate are found for RPC with higher SP dosages. The high SP dosage would cause changes in the pore structure which inﬂuence the state of the evaporable water (or physical bound water) and consequently the shrinkage behaviour. Mixes with retardation or high SP dosage, both autogenous shrinkage and evaporation increased, drying shrinkage is increased and thus the crack tendency [50]. High SP dosage will lead to increase in mass loss because of the continuous increase of water loss or evaporation [55]. 5.1.3. Methods for reducing shrinkage It has been reported that concrete with low water-to-binder ra- tio, high cement content, addition of superplasticizer and incorpo- ration of silica fume, which are the characteristics of RPC, creates a water starved mixture and results in an extremely dense micro- structure within a few days or less [56]. This rapid development of
